issue_title: CONTRIBUTING.md is misleading
issue_body:
[CONTRIBUTING.rst](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.rst) is kind of confusing, specifically the part about using tox to create an environment.

Someone had problems using tox -r --notest -e py27 to "poke around", as far as I can tell skip_install is true so the environment is not actually created from the root of the repo. Maybe it meant to do so only from a specific package?

I also think since many tox files don't actually install the setup.py deps, it's confusing to suggest using tox to install things, probably better just to use pip.

issue_title: Passing interpreter when referencing modules on PYTHONPATH
Improve performance related to content addressability
issue_body:
@stuhood  did profiling for `./pants run src/python/pants/engine/exp/legacy:dependencies -- ::`, looks like we spend significant amount of time pickling.

There are some low hanging fruits like 

* caching the computed keys so we don't repeatedly pickle the same object to get its key. 
* Also lmdb high miss rate (`step_id` should not be included in the fingerprint) may also contributed.

While that helps `get`, we probably still need to be smart about what to content address for `put`.

We know in minimum we need to cache `task_node`, now we cache everything, i.e, 100% in keys. We will need a representation that's compatible with both keys and content.

Best explained by a custom plugin build file alias I just wrote for Aurora:
```python
def build_file_aliases():
  # This should ship as part of pants core - any custom plugin writer will want to have their
  # plugin depend on the same version of pants as the one their running.  
  pants_requirement = PythonRequirement('pantsbuild.pants==%s' % pants_version())
  return BuildFileAliases(objects={'pants_requirement': pants_requirement})
```

issue_title: Design new PEX entrypoints to expose PEXBuilder
issue_body:
A core portion of the work accomplished by the `gather_sources.py` and `resolve_requirements_task_base.py` files is collecting their inputs into PEXBuilders.

Currently we build two(ish) pexes: one for the sources, and one for the requirements... and in both cases this is accomplished by usage of the `PEXBuilder.add_*` methods (for `sources`, `resources`, `requirements`).

In order to create mergeable [0] pexes from sources and requirements using `@rule`s, we should expose an API similar to PEXBuilder directly as a CLI tool. As a strawman, this might look like:
```
pex-builder \
  --source=com/blah/foo.py \
  --source=com/fuzz/bar.py \
  --resource=com/blah/blah.txt \
  --requirement='libbar=1.2.3' \
  "${dest_file}.pex"
```

[0] We also need to decide whether we merge the resulting pex files using `PEX_PATH` or something similar to the pex merging API from `ResolveRequirementsTaskBase.merged_pex`.

issue_title: Removing messages from .proto files leaves stale sources when `option java_multiple_files = true` is set.
issue_body:
We've experienced an issue when users update a .proto file to remove a definition when the .proto is defined to use `option java_multiple_files = true`.  Here is a repro case:

```
diff --git a/examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/protobuf/distance/ExampleProtobuf.java b/examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/protobuf/distance/ExampleProtobuf.java
index 0eabc28..9226df4 100644
--- a/examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/protobuf/distance/ExampleProtobuf.java
+++ b/examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/protobuf/distance/ExampleProtobuf.java
@@ -3,7 +3,7 @@
 
 package org.pantsbuild.example.protobuf.distance;
 
-import org.pantsbuild.example.distance.Distances;
+import org.pantsbuild.example.distance.Distance;
 
 class ExampleProtobuf {
 
@@ -11,6 +11,6 @@ class ExampleProtobuf {
   }
 
   public static void main(String[] args) {
-      System.out.println(Distances.Distance.newBuilder().setNumber(12).setUnit("parsecs").build());
+      System.out.println(Distance.newBuilder().setNumber(12).setUnit("parsecs").build());
   }
 }
diff --git a/examples/src/protobuf/org/pantsbuild/example/distance/distances.proto b/examples/src/protobuf/org/pantsbuild/example/distance/distances.proto
index 591aa39..c88ead7 100644
--- a/examples/src/protobuf/org/pantsbuild/example/distance/distances.proto
+++ b/examples/src/protobuf/org/pantsbuild/example/distance/distances.proto
@@ -3,6 +3,8 @@
 
 package org.pantsbuild.example.distance;
 
+option java_multiple_files = true;
+
 /**
  * Structure for expressing distance measures: 8mm, 12 parsecs, etc.
  * Not so useful on its own.
@@ -11,3 +13,9 @@ message Distance {
   optional string unit = 1;
   required int64 number = 2;
 }
+
+message Foo {
+  optional string field = 1;
+}
```

Apply the above patch and run

```
./pants compile examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/protobuf/distance
```

Now, edit distance.proto and comment out the definition for `message Foo`.  Now run the compile again and it will fail:

```

What's happening here is that the `message Foo` definition generates a new file `.pants.d/gen/protoc/gen-java/org/pantsbuild/example/distance/Foo.java`.  When you remove the definition there is nothing in pants to clean up the generated file, but some of the symbols it refers to in another file `Distances.java` are removed when that file is regenerated.

The workaround is to run `./pants clean-all`

issue_title: Python tests should be run in chroots to enforce proper loose file dependency declaration.
issue_body:
#4780 handled the JVM side of this, but as far as I can tell the pytest runner does not actually change CWD into the chroot, so it is still possible to access loose files (as demonstrated by https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/tests/python/pants_test/pants_run_integration_test.py subclasses not actually needing any `files(..)` target dependencies to succeed). Having undeclared dependencies caused master breakage due to cached tests, and so test caching was disabled in travis in #5016.

This ticket covers adding the option to change working directories, and hopefully using it somewhere in pantsbuild/pants CI... the unit test shards seem like a good target, as they don't require tackling the integration test dependencies.

issue_title: Non-string label values cause export-time failures
issue_body:
Label values are not checked for string-likeness, and are not explicitly converted to strings. This causes the following problems:

```python
c = Counter('http_requests_total_by_method', 'Count of requests by HTTP method.', ['method']

def handle_request(request):
  c.labels(request.method).inc()
```

to fail silently if request.method is None (or a tuple, or an object, etc.). At export time, the following will happen:

```
Exception happened during processing of request from ('127.0.0.1', 55703)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 295, in _handle_request_noblock
    self.process_request(request, client_address)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 321, in process_request
    self.finish_request(request, client_address)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 334, in finish_request
    self.RequestHandlerClass(request, client_address, self)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 651, in __init__
    self.handle()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/BaseHTTPServer.py", line 340, in handle
    self.handle_one_request()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/BaseHTTPServer.py", line 328, in handle_one_request
    method()
  File "/.../env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/prometheus_client-0.0.7-py2.7.egg/prometheus_client/__init__.py", line 404, in do_GET
    self.wfile.write(generate_latest(REGISTRY))
  File ".../env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/prometheus_client-0.0.7-py2.7.egg/prometheus_client/__init__.py", line 392, in generate_latest
    for k, v in labels.items()]))
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'replace'
```

There are several ways around this:
* Raise a ValueError in `c.labels()` if the label values are not all string-like
* In `c.labels()` or in `generate_latest`, coerce all label values to the unicode (or string) type
* In `generate_latest`, drop labels that can't be rendered, but render the other labels for that metric.
* In `generate_latest`, drop metrics that can't be rendered, but render the rest.

I'm not a fan of the options that drop data, because there's no worse monitoring than monitoring that lies to you. I think coercing to string may work, but I'd prefer if the code could raise. It already raises if the code provides the wrong number of label values, so raising on the wrong type of label values isn't unexpected.

issue_title: SAML Frontend always return NameID with format persistent
issue_body:
Function 

``` python
def hash_type_to_saml_name_id_format(hash_type):
    """
    Translate satosa format to pySAML2 name format

    :type hash_type: satosa.internal_data.UserIdHashType
    :rtype: str
    :param hash_type: satosa format
    :return: pySAML2 name format
    """
    if hash_type == UserIdHashType.transient.name:
        return NAMEID_FORMAT_TRANSIENT
    elif hash_type == UserIdHashType.persistent.name:
        return NAMEID_FORMAT_PERSISTENT
    return NAMEID_FORMAT_PERSISTENT

```

in https://github.com/SUNET/SATOSA/blob/master/src/satosa/frontends/saml2.py#L31

compares `hash_type` which is a UserIdHashType object to ` UserIdHashType.transient.name` which is a string. This comparison is always False, hence the default return is always followed and the SAML2 frontent always creates an assertion with NameID format persistent in the Subject.

issue_title: Add check for maximum URL length of a request
issue_body:
Currently working with the Elevations API, and I noticed that some of my bigger requests (single request, lots of points) returned with a HTTPError, status code 400. After checking the  [API docs](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/elevation/intro#ElevationRequests) and some testing, this seems to be an URL length issue: request URLs are limited to "about 2000 characters, after URL Encoding".

It seems that the `googlemaps` module (or at least the `Client`'s `elevation()` method does not perform a check on this URL length. My only option, currently, is to find the "sweet spot" in the number of points per request that will make sure the URL length stays below the limit, and use that number to split up my Elevation API requests.

Is auto-handling URL length something that could be built in?

issue_title: Warn users when they attempt to re-import an already-installed channel
issue_body:
As a user, I would like to be informed that a channel that I intend to importing from a remote or local source is already installed on my device before starting any data transfer.

## Scenario

### Remote source
Given that channel 'foo' with channel id `f00` is installed on my device
When I go through the 'import remote channel' workflow and attempt to download `f00`
Then I am notified that the channel is already there
Then I can continue with the download or choose to cancel.

### Local source
Given that channel 'foo' with channel id `f00` is installed on my device
Given that channel 'foo' (and potentially other channels) is installed on my USB drive
When I go through the 'import local channel(s)' workflow and attempt to import the USB drive contents
Then I am notified that 'foo' channel is already there
Then I can continue with the download or choose to cancel.


## Summary

I tried to import a channel with the same ID as a channel already loaded and did not receive an error or warning message. I'm not sure if the channel ever re-imported. I assume that the new content from the ID will replace the old content but: 
(1) You should receive a warning message confirming that this is what you want to do (especially in a context where it is a large file and the internet connection isn't strong); or
(2) You should be prompted to delete the old ID first (which may not be necessary if it will just replace it anyway; in case there's a problem importing the new content, at least the old will be there?)

## System information

*Please specify the Kolibri version you were using and your operating system!*

 - Version: 0.4.0beta5.dev244 - APK
 - Operating system: Android 6.0.1 (on Samsung S5)
 - Browser: n/a (Android App)

## How to reproduce

1. Install Android App, log in, and go to Manage tab.
2. Import Channel ID (I was using 93a5bfcfa2a74962be843288aefcfc0e)
3. Once content has been loaded, try reimporting the same channel ID and observe.

## Real-life consequences (anything community should be aware of, for instance how it affects your deployment)

I could see this being problematic if you are in a low-bandwidth context and the filesize is large and this is accidental. It would be helpful for Kolibri to acknowledge that you're trying to do this. In my opinion from a user experience perspective, this wouldn't interrupt my experience, but rather reassure me that it's the correct path forward.

issue_title: Channel file/resource stats on Content Mgmt Page not always accurate

Currently, when you download a channel, but it does not finish (e.g. server disconnection), the row on the Content Management page gives you Resource and Total File Size numbers based on the full size of the channel (e.g. as if 100% downloaded). So if a 1000-resource channel were only 50% downloaded, it would still say '1000 resources', not '500 resources'.

issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Currently, searching results in some very unexpected behaviors. It seems to be "fuzzy" but in the wrong ways - e.g. it will pick up on completely unrelated content due to spelling mappings, while not finding synonyms of common words.

### Expected behavior

Search should at least be predictable: if something appears in the results, it should be obvious why it's there. This will encourage people to trust and use it.

As a simple starting point, we should remove the behavior which causes results to appear based on complex character mappings.

This will reduce the number of results for each search query, but make what does get returned more likely to be relevant to the user.

### User-facing consequences

Search is confusing and misleading because it often returns completely unrelated content


-----


cc @christianmemije @ralphiee22

issue_title: IPv6 route nexthop not updating
Add support for asyncio
issue_body:
It would be great to support [asyncio](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio.html).

Asyncio socket class implementation (depends on #25) with sample usage below. This is bit hacky implementation
- `send` and `recv` methods are coroutines
- file descriptors of SP sockets are watched with `loop.add_reader` and `loop.add_writer`
- also `Socket.poll` is used to double check that data can be received from or sent to the SP socket
- if `Socket.poll` tells that it is not possible to receive/send data without blocking, then the hackery starts

Should `nnpy` support `asyncio`? Is API based on `SocketAsync` class OK?

```
import asyncio
import nnpy
from nnpy.constants import SNDFD, RCVFD, SOL_SOCKET
from nnpy import nanomsg, ffi

class SocketAsync(nnpy.Socket):
    def __init__(self, domain, protocol, loop=None):
        super().__init__(domain, protocol)
        if loop == None:
            loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self._loop = loop

        self._reader = asyncio.Queue(loop=loop)
        self._fd_reader = None
        self._read_flags = 0

        self._writer = asyncio.Event(loop=loop)
        self._fd_writer = None
        self._write_flags = 0

    async def recv(self, flags=0):
        self._read_flags = flags
        self._enable_reader()
        value = await self._reader.get()
        return value

    async def send(self, data, flags=0):
        self._data = data
        self._write_flags = flags
        self._enable_writer()
        await self._writer.wait()
                
    def _notify_recv(self):
        self._loop.remove_reader(self._fd_reader)
            
        if self.poll(1):
            result = super().recv(self._read_flags)                                                                    
            self._reader.put_nowait(result)
        else:
            self._loop.call_later(0.5, self._enable_reader)                                                            
        
    def _notify_send(self):
        self._loop.remove_writer(self._fd_writer)
        
        if self.poll(2):
            super().send(self._data, self._write_flags)
            self._writer.set()                                                                                         
            self._writer.clear()
        else:
            self._loop.call_later(0.5, self._enable_writer)
    
    def _enable_reader(self):
        self._fd_reader = self.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, RCVFD)                                                           
        self._loop.add_reader(self._fd_reader, self._notify_recv) 
                   
    def _enable_writer(self):                                                                                          
        self._fd_writer = self.getsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SNDFD)                                                           
        self._loop.add_writer(self._fd_writer, self._notify_send)   

async def reader(sub):
    while True:                                                                                                        
        print('receiving...')
        value = await sub.recv()
        print('received:', value)
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

async def writer(pub):
    i = 0
    while True:
        print('sending...')
        value = '{:04}'.format(i)
        await pub.send(value)
        print('sent', value)
        i += 1
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

sub = SocketAsync(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.PULL)
sub.bind('tcp://*:5555')
pub = SocketAsync(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.PUSH)
pub.connect('tcp://localhost:5555')

loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(writer(pub), reader(sub)))
```

I noticed that a failed build doesn't throw an exception or fail in any way. This is in contrast with the command line client that will exit non-zero on a build failure. I'd like to be able to catch and exception on build failure.

What is a good way to figure out build status currently?

issue_title: docker-py running container blocks access to "docker stats"
issue_body:
I am using docker-py  to run a short-running (1m-2h) container in the background and I'd like to monitor that container's stats to record and characterize its I/O, CPU, and memory usage every second or two.  But it appears that while containers are running under docker-py I can't get stats, either from my code through docker-py or the command line "docker stats".  Streaming or no streaming, both seem to just hang until they timeout, or the container just happens to finish and unblock docker.

I'm using Ubuntu 14.04, docker-py version 1.9.0, and docker version 1.12.1, plain vanilla from the repos, connecting over a local domain socket as configured by default (/var/run/docker.sock).

issue_title: production name setting in the Glyphs file should take precedent over the fontmake option
issue_body: production name setting in the Glyphs file should take precedent over the fontmake option and we should use --no-production-names or --production-names when this info is not in the Glyphs file. IMHO, we should give warning if what in the Glyphs file is different from the fontmake option

issue_title: difference between the hb-view outputs for font generated by pipeline and by the Glyphs app
issue_body:
I used  [NotoSerifThai-MM.glyphs.zip](https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake/files/176455/NotoSerifThai-MM.glyphs.zip) as input to both pipeline and to the Glyphs.app on Mac.

The pipeline produced the following OTF font
[NotoSerifThai-Regular.otf.zip](https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake/files/176478/NotoSerifThai-Regular.otf.zip)

The Glyphs.app on Mac produced the following OTF font
[NotoSerifThai-Regular.otf.zip](https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake/files/176457/NotoSerifThai-Regular.otf.zip)

Then I ran
hb-view NotoSerifThai-Regular.otf --output-format=png --text-file=../thai-sample2.txt > ts.png
for each of the fonts.

Pipeline font produced the following image
![ts2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/13322623/13820962/efe6c976-eb5c-11e5-8a07-8ba0da6f8f46.png)

The Glyphs.app on Mac font produced the following image
![ts](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/13322623/13821008/1373400e-eb5d-11e5-9b1d-0d7c815dee7d.png)

Somehow I think the Glyphs.app on Mac font is a better font :-)

issue_title: Looks like version still shows as "0.3.0" for 0.5.0
issue_body: Seems NRFUTIL_VERSION = "0.3.0" is not updated.

issue_title: Service changed indication will always fail.
issue_body: Service changed indication upon re-connection will always raise an exception since the on_gattc_evt_hvx() function inside ble_adapter.py gets the uuid of the indication from self.db_conns, which got deleted upon disconnection.

issue_title: Allow array slicing
Incorporate NI-SWITCH system tests into NI internal Automated Test Suite
issue_body:
NI maintains an Automated Test Suite for the driver.
It needs to install the corresponding nimi-python package and run the corresponding system tests as part of its execution.

This way we can ensure that future versions of the driver work with nimi-python.

issue_title: niscope.Session.cal_self_calibrate() is inconsistent
Warnings returned by the driver should not raise
issue_body:
If the underlying driver runtime returns a warning, as it stands today, the Python binding raises.
One example of this is when a DMM reads over-range. It is undesirable to change flow control of the program in these scenarios.

[nidaqmx](https://github.com/ni/nidaqmx-python) handles warnings by logging them to file. We should look into their approach and deal with warnings in the same manner.

We should also log when driver's close function errors.

issue_title: get_library() creates a new object on every call
issue_body:
Every time you open a session to the API, get_library is called. This returns a new object.
It should be smarter and return a delay-created singleton, for efficiency.

issue_title: Counter Resetting Every Read
issue_body:
I am using a USB-6009 multifunction DAQ with Windows 10 and Python 3.6 as a digital counter on the PFI0 port. The counter tests good in NI MAX. Also, if I read multiple samples, the counter does increment as expected when presented with a 1 kHz square wave. 

However, I find that the counter state is reset before every read operation. In other words, if I read 1 sample at a time, I will get the value "0" or "1" as an output. If I read, for example, 1000 samples at once, each sample (more or less, depending on sampling rate) is incrementing as expected. 

I am using the example code in ci_count_edges.py, which uses i_channels.add_ci_count_edges_chan().

The underlying NI DAQ MX function is DAQmxReadCounterU32Ex(), which isn't supposed to do this. Therefore, the cause is a bit of a mystery to me.

issue_title: Deprecated Fixer.io API
issue_body:
The Fixer.io API currently in use by forex-python is deprecated and will stop working on June 1st. The new API requires a key, free keys only allow 1000 requests/month and EUR as the only base currency.

See the announcement on their GitHub repo: https://github.com/fixerAPI/fixer#readme

issue_body:

Mark

issue_title: TokenCharactersEncoder Cannot Handle 4 Dimensional Input
issue_body:
Here's the error message:
```
  File "allennlp/allennlp/models/encoder_decoders/wikitables_semantic_parser.py", line 183, in forward
    embedded_table = self._question_embedder({'tokens': tokens_tensor, 'token_characters': token_characters_tensor})
  File "allennlp/allennlp/modules/text_field_embedders/basic_text_field_embedder.py", line 49, in forward
    token_vectors = embedder(tensor)
  File "allennlp/allennlp/modules/token_embedders/token_characters_encoder.py", line 36, in forward
    return self._dropout(self._encoder(self._embedding(token_characters), mask))
  File "allennlp/allennlp/modules/time_distributed.py", line 35, in forward
    reshaped_outputs = self._module(*reshaped_inputs)
  File "allennlp/allennlp/modules/seq2vec_encoders/cnn_encoder.py", line 106, in forward
    self._activation(convolution_layer(tokens)).max(dim=2)[0]
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/conv.py", line 166, in forward
    self.padding, self.dilation, self.groups)
  File "lib/python3.6/site-packages/torch/nn/functional.py", line 49, in conv1d
    raise ValueError("Expected 3D tensor as input, got {}D tensor instead.".format(input.dim()))
ValueError: Expected 3D tensor as input, got 4D tensor instead.
```

Background:
When doing characters embeddings for the wikitables dataset, the input vector is 4 dimensional.
`(batch_size, num_entities, num_entity_tokens, num_entity_token_characters)`

When the vector reaches this line:

https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/40dcf53c57464a51488a86e58fa583aff3058d44/allennlp/modules/token_embedders/token_characters_encoder.py#L36
The embedder adds another dimension to make it:
`(batch_size, num_entities, num_entity_tokens, num_entity_token_characters, character_embedding_dim)`

When it is passed to the CNN Encoder the first two dimensions (batch_size, num_entities) are collapsed by the time distributed to a 4D vector which results in this error as CNN encoder expects a 3D vector.
 
It would be nice if token_characters_encoder squished the vector to 3 dimensions before passing it to the CNN encoder. That way I can experiment with character or word embeddings without having to modify code.

Also, here's the relevant section from the config file:
```
  "question_embedder": {
      "tokens": {
        "type": "embedding",
        "embedding_dim": 25,
        "trainable": true
      },
      "token_characters": {
        "type": "character_encoding",
        "embedding": {
          "num_embeddings": 262,
          "embedding_dim": 16
        },
        "encoder": {
          "type": "cnn",
          "embedding_dim": 16,
          "num_filters": 100,
          "ngram_filter_sizes": [3]
        },
        "dropout": 0.2
    }
```

issue_title: allure.issue and allure.testcase require both url and name params contradicting documentation
issue_body:
[//]: # (
. Note: for support questions, please use Stackoverflow or Gitter**. 
. This repository's issues are reserved for feature requests and bug reports.
.
. In case of any problems with Allure Jenkins plugin** please use the following repository 
. to create an issue: https://github.com/jenkinsci/allure-plugin/issues
.
. Make sure you have a clear name for your issue. The name should start with a capital 
. letter and no dot is required in the end of the sentence. An example of good issue names:
.
. - The report is broken in IE11
. - Add an ability to disable default plugins
. - Support emoji in test descriptions
)

#### I'm submitting a ... 
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request
  - [ ] support request => Please do not submit support request here, see note at the top of this template.

#### What is the current behavior?
Implementation of `allure.issue` and `allure.testcase` decorators in `pytest-allure-adaptor` only required one argument e.g. the URL or name of test/issue.

In `allure-pytest` this behavior has changed and now requires both arguments `url` and `name` supplied. On top of that `name` argument from function declaration seems to be optional which is not entirely true.

```python
def testcase(url, name=None):
    return link(url, link_type=LinkType.TEST_CASE, name=name)
```

#### If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce and if possible a minimal demo of the problem

Non-working example with `allure-pytest` (works fine with `pytest-allure-adaptor`)

```python
import allure

@allure.issue('http://localhost/JIRA-001')
@allure.testcase('http://localhost/TC-001')
def test_first():
    pass
```
Resulting report has missing issue and testcase links in report **Links** section.

#### What is the expected behavior?

Issue and test case links are available in report as specified in decorators.

#### What is the motivation / use case for changing the behavior?

As I see it there are 2 options to approach this:

1. If the new behavior was intended then this has to be documented and `name` argument should be made mandatory.

1. Change behavior to match what is [docuemnted](https://docs.qameta.io/allure/2.0/#_issues_tracker) and matches behavior in `pytest-allure-adaptor`.

#### Please tell us about your environment:

- Allure version:        2.4.1
- Test framework:        pytest@pytest-3.2.3
- Allure adaptor:        allure-pytest@2.2.3b

#### Other information 

[//]: # (
. e.g. detailed explanation, stacktraces, related issues, suggestions 
. how to fix, links for us to have more context, eg. Stackoverflow, Gitter etc
)

issue_title: Report shows wrong attachments for parameterized fixtures
issue_body:
#### I'm submitting a ... 
  - [x] bug report

#### What is the current behavior?
Report shows wrong attachments for parameterized fixtures

#### If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce and if possible a minimal demo of the problem
Simple test to run as an example of the bug:
```
import allure
import pytest
import time

@pytest.fixture(scope="module", params=["a","b","c","d","e","f"])
def letter(request):
    timestamp = time.ctime()
    allure.attach(str(timestamp), "Log", attachment_type=allure.attachment_type.TEXT)
    print(timestamp)
    return request.param

def test_letter(letter):
    time.sleep(1)
    assert True
```

On pytest output you can see three different timestamps with one sec difference from each other, like:
```
'a': Mon Feb 12 17:40:07 2018
'b' :Mon Feb 12 17:40:08 2018
'c': Mon Feb 12 17:40:09 2018
'd': Mon Feb 12 17:40:10 2018
'e': Mon Feb 12 17:40:11 2018
'f': Mon Feb 12 17:40:12 2018
```
But on the Logs, you can see it prints on set up, for each of the tests:
```
'a': Mon Feb 12 17:40:07 2018
'b' :Mon Feb 12 17:40:07 2018
'c': Mon Feb 12 17:40:08 2018
'd': Mon Feb 12 17:40:09 2018
'e': Mon Feb 12 17:40:10 2018
'f': Mon Feb 12 17:40:11 2018
```
So it repeats the setup attachment for letter 'b' and then keep with the sequence (therefore of course attachments are wrong on all tests).

#### What is the expected behavior?

Attachments should belong to the fixture instance it belongs to.

#### Please tell us about your environment:

- Allure version:        2.5.0
- Test framework:        pytest@3.4.0

issue_title: utilities.helpers.make_valid_url breaks some urls
issue_body:
# Code

```python
def make_valid_url(url):
    """Make sure url doesn't contain unsupported characters

    Args:
        * `url`: a url string
    """
    if '/+/http' in url:
        glue = '/+/'
        quoted = [make_valid_url(unquoted) for unquoted in url.split(glue)]
        return (glue).join(quoted)

    scheme, netloc, path, query, fragment = compat.urlsplit(url)
    quoted_path = compat.quote(path.encode('utf-8'))
    quoted_query = compat.quote_plus(query.encode('utf-8'))
    new_url_tuple = (scheme, netloc, quoted_path, quoted_query, fragment)
    quoted_url = compat.urlunsplit(new_url_tuple)
    return quoted_url
```

# Example

Before this function (OK-200):
```
http://next.openspending.org/fdp-adapter/convert?url=https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Fkravets-levko%2Fdata%2Fmaster%2Ftest.xlsx.csv

After this function (ISE-500):

```

issue_title: solvers.py: solve_CBC() crashes when white space in location
issue_body:
Line 1419 of solvers.py contains the following expression:
`cbc = subprocess.Popen((self.path + cmds).split(), stdout = pipe,
                             stderr = pipe)`

This code will crash if self.path contains a white space character. Normally, this should not be the case but it can happen. One example is the usage of py2app or py2exe, which bundles pulp. Moving the .app or .exe to a directory with a white space in the path, will crash the app.

issue_title: Consider using Terraform for Azure provisioner
issue_body:
Terraform isn't quite there yet with regard to feature parity with our current Azure provision scripts:
* Terraform doesn't support load balancers (https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform/issues/3055)
* Terraform doesn't support defining data disks on instance creation (https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform/issues/3054)

issue_title: Cli tool for release process
issue_body:
Right now our deis release process involves following steps on a spreadsheet. That's spreadsheet-driven-deployment! And it makes me :cry:.

@mboersma had the idea of creating a cli tool that could automate most of the steps:
- git process (tags, pushes, etc.)
- jenkins process (kicking off builds, waiting for them, etc.)
- doc process (wait for a human to add release notes, configure human readable docs where appropriate)

We could eventually move this (or part of it) into a Jenkins job to benefit from being kicked off by an upstream part of a release pipeline, but we gotta start somewhere to codify our release process in a more concrete fashion.

issue_title: bookmark rename & delete: allow use of name or id
issue_body:
Currently bookmark rename and delete require a bookmark id, but cannot take a bookmark name:
Usage: globus bookmark rename [OPTIONS] BOOKMARK_ID NEW_BOOKMARK_NAME
Usage: globus bookmark delete [OPTIONS] BOOKMARK_ID

Change them to allow wither a bookmark id or name, like bookmark show allows it:
Usage: globus bookmark show [OPTIONS] BOOKMARK_ID_OR_NAME

So change them to:
Usage: globus bookmark rename [OPTIONS] BOOKMARK_ID_OR_NAME NEW_BOOKMARK_NAME
Usage: globus bookmark delete [OPTIONS] BOOKMARK_ID_OR_NAME

issue_title: Allow `VM` class to specify what `ChainDB` class it uses.
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

In #350 the differences between shard collations and blocks required a bit of a hack to make the `persist_block_to_db` to work with both blocks and collations.

While we *could* try to sufficiently abstract the API, such as separating things into `persist_block_to_db` and `persist_collation_to_db`, I don't believe this is the right solution.  Eventually, we are bound to have either blocks, transactions, or something that require more such logic forks to handle.

Py-EVM has so far largely avoided these types of logic forks in it's code by sufficiently abstracting the overall API so that underlying implementations can be swapped out within each VM.

### How can it be fixed

I think that the `Chain` class should be modified so that it takes a backend `DB` type, and then delegates to the active `VM` for the appropriate block number to get the `ChainDB`.  Each `VM` class can then define their own `ChainDB` class.  Then the `ShardVM` class can expose it's own `ChainDB` class with modified `persist_block_to_db` method.

issue_title: Add a transaction by hash lookup
issue_body:
Need to support a transaction lookup by hash for the json-rpc method `eth_getTransactionByHash`.

First impression for an approach:
- add transactions to the key-value store (even pending transactions should be retrievable)
- key = hash, value = transaction root & index (so that we can reuse logic/data in `chaindb.get_block_transactions` and don't store the whole transaction twice in the db)
- need to add transactions in these scenarios
  - import block
  - create transaction locally
  - ?

Alternatives:
- Store the transaction body once with a key of the transaction hash, and store the transaction hash under the key= transaction root & index (the reverse of the suggestion above, since transaction lookup by hash might be more common and the cost of a double lookup might be high)

Open questions
- some kind of garbage cleanup for pending transactions that never get included in a block

issue_title: Only try updating outdated packages with update --all
issue_body:
conda update --all tends to fail a lot because it requires that the whole environment become satisfiable, and without downgrading any packages. Perhaps a better solution would be to only try installing those packages that are known to be outdated. 

Another idea would be to relax the downgrade restriction, and just have it essentially "reinstall" the environment. This could lead to some surprises when it does downgrade things, but it would also reduce the number of unsatisfiable packages issues, as those seem to usually come from the version specification.

issue_title: confusing messages at downgrading
issue_body:
When I willfully downgrade a package, confusing dialogs are given related to dependency conflicts, possibly because the same dialog/logging is used when an automatic downgrade requirement was being determined:

```bash
(stable) └─❱❱❱ conda install pandas=0.19                                        +6295 8:05 ❰─┘
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: ..........

Package plan for installation in environment /Users/klay6683/miniconda3/envs/stable:

The following packages will be DOWNGRADED due to dependency conflicts:

    pandas: 0.20.1-np112py36_0 conda-forge --> 0.19.2-np112py36_1 conda-forge

Proceed ([y]/n)?
```

I think it would be preferable, that in the deliberate downgrade the messages should not say `due to dependency conflicts` and that this part is only added to the dialog when a dependency conflict was automatically found.

version: 4.2.13 on macOS

issue_title: Creating a new environment with a tarball provides a confusing message
issue_body:
When you create an environment with a tarball the code flow is this:

* Unpack tarball
* Link package into environment
* Check for dependencies
* Display success message stating no packages were installed

It's extremely confusing as it appears the package you installed did not make it in there.

**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior

When installing a corrupted package, the verify step of conda hangs for a long time without telling anything.

When running the same with `-v -v -v` one can see errors of type: 

```
TRACE conda.core.path_actions:verify(269): retrying lexists(C:\Miniconda3\pkgs\boost-1.61.0-vc14h6b12553_2\Library\bin\boost_atomic-vc140-mt-1_61.dll) in 0.141842 sec

DEBUG conda.core.link:_verify_individual_level(337): Verification error in action LinkPathAction(target_prefix='C:\\Miniconda3', target_short_path='Library/bin/boost_atomic-vc140-mt-gd-1_61.dll', source_short_path='Library/bin/boost_atomic-vc140-mt-gd-1_61.dll', source_path_data=PathDataV1(_path='Library/bin/boost_atomic-vc140-mt-gd-1_61.dll', path_type='hardlink', sha256='df13333e7f1d40aa29fc0193a62d58d730691b09a0d291c981514206db9d25cd', size_in_bytes=16896), source_prefix='C:\\Miniconda3\\pkgs\\boost-1.61.0-vc14h6b12553_2', _execute_successful=False, link_type=<LinkType.hardlink: 1>, prefix_path_data=None)

specified in the package manifest cannot be found.
``` 

So what happens is: conda finds out that the linking doesn't seem to work for a file, it retries this file for 3.2 seconds, fails and then continues with the next file - which also fails. 

Now as you can see, the package is boost and it has 12475 files. This would go on for about 10-12hours, without saying anything to the user.

We could not really reproduce why the package was corrupted in the first place but it seems after we cleaned the cache it worked..

### Steps to Reproduce
```
conda install <a package with missing files>
```


## Expected Behavior

Conda should stop after the first verification error, maybe rollback and tell the user that verification failed.

-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
```

Whereas in a local compiled version (Linux/gcc 5.4.0), I am getting this:

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import bcolz

In [3]: a = np.arange(1e8)

In [4]: %timeit ca = bcolz.carray(a, cparams=bcolz.cparams(cname='lz4'))
10 loops, best of 3: 104 ms per loop

In [5]: bcolz.print_versions()
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
bcolz version:     1.0.0
bcolz git info:    b'1.0.0'
NumPy version:     1.11.1
Blosc version:     1.8.1 ($Date:: 2016-04-08 #$)

Numexpr version:   2.6.1.dev0

[GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)]
Platform:          linux-x86_64
Byte-ordering:     little
Detected cores:    8
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
```

which is a 1.7x faster than the version in Anaconda.

My hunch is that Anaconda is compiled (for compatibility reasons) with a relatively old compiler (judging by the logs above gcc 4.4.7?), which looses a lot of the advantages that optimizers in newer compilers are offering.  I am wondering if this could be solved somehow?  Thanks!

issue_title: ``deactivate`` adds miniconda/bin from path
issue_body:
I have just installed miniconda3 for testing purposes. When installing I've said **not** to add the path to miniconda3/bin to the ``.bashrc`` file.

I've created an environment and activated.

    source miniconda3/bin/activate my_env
    discarding /home/user/miniconda3/bin from PATH  # which I don't have
    prepending /home/user/miniconda3/envs/my_env/bin to PATH

When I deactivated it it told me 

    discarding /home/user/miniconda3/envs/conda_hetdex/bin from PATH

but it silently added the path to miniconda3/bin, messing up my environment.

Since I asked not to add miniconda to my path, I would call it a bug.
Upon activation, conda should remember whether miniconda3/bin is on the path. When deactivating it should read it only if it was already there. I guess that it would be something similar to the way PS1 is saved in the CONDA_OLD_PS1 environment variable.

issue_body: Conda may try to clean up some trash as a background operation when running other commands, but it probably shouldn't sleep or wait doing so, in the event the cleaning fails.

issue_title: Consider allowing conda to install python sdists
issue_body:
There isn't a technical reason I know of that conda shouldn't be able to install non-compiler-invoking universal-type python sdist packages with well-formed setup.py files. We'd probably want to follow the pip example here, where pip downloads the sdist, creates a wheel locally, archives it in a cache, and then installs the wheel.

So this proposal requires conda-build along with conda. I think the flow would be for conda to ask conda-build to download the sdist, build a conda package for it, and then conda can install it from the local channel. 

This wouldn't work 100% of the time. It probably wouldn't work reliably for any packages that have extension modules/invoke a compiler. Support for the various python versions also adds extra complexity, so an initial implementation might target this only for packages that are "universal" python packages.

While it won't work 100% of the time, it might work 70% or 80% of the time. And for that extra 20%-30%, most of those are probably the "hard" packages that we've spent building up in repo.continuum.io anyway.

issue_title: Expose auto-ping data
issue_body:
Currently the auto-ping feature sends empty pings, and there is no explicit way of setting the ping data. This is fine as a keep-alive, but the developer may want to send a ping with data -- as a workaround for broken servers (https://github.com/wildfoundry/dataplicity-lomond/issues/75) or other reasons.

Consider some kind of hook mechanism to allow a custom function to be called to get the data for a ping packet.

issue_title: VUnit python interface: "getting" a Library
issue_body:
# Problem
The [vunit.ui.VUnit.library](https://vunit.github.io/python_interface.html#vunit.ui.VUnit.library) method returns [new instances of a library](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/b5b9b2498a9150632b3ea0f8f701526fdc5f1c32/vunit/ui.py#L416). I understand that this is just an interface into the underlying `vunit.project.Library` class, but this becomes an issue when trying to access previously added libraries on an instance of VUnit programatically. For a bit more context, I am setting up a larger test suite using the pattern @LarsAsplund lays out on [SO](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42980036/how-to-combine-multiple-vunit-run-py-files-into-a-single-vunit-run). When doing this I need to "get" Libraries I may have previously created and added files to so I can add additional files.

# Reproduction
```python
from vunit import VUnit
from os.path import dirname, abspath, join

# create the file we try to add
open('file.vhd', 'a').close()

proj_root = dirname(abspath(__file__))
vu = VUnit.from_argv()

try:
    test_lib = vu.library('Test')
except KeyError:
    print('Test Library does not exist, creating it now.')
    test_lib = vu.add_library('Test')

test_lib_2 = vu.library('Test')

print('Test Library returned after initial creation       : {0} - {1}'.format(test_lib.name, test_lib))
print('Test Library returned after "getting" it from VUnit: {0} - {1}'.format(test_lib_2.name, test_lib_2))

test_lib.add_source_file(join(proj_root, 'file.vhd'))
test_lib_2.add_source_file(join(proj_root, 'file.vhd'))

# Cannot access file from because we it now exists in two "libraries"
test_lib.get_source_file('file.vhd')
```

[2018-03-05T04:03:44,897][WARN ][o.e.s.SnapshotsService   ] [0AB1Ezt] [repo:snapshot-20180305035936_<redacted>/2OKxaLkfSIm-Te5TWkamWQ] failed to finalize snapshot
org.elasticsearch.repositories.RepositoryException: [repo] concurrent modification of the index-N file, expected current generation [1290], actual current generation [1293] - possibly due to simultaneous snapshot deletion requests
        at org.elasticsearch.repositories.blobstore.BlobStoreRepository.writeIndexGen(BlobStoreRepository.java:825) ~[elasticsearch-5.5.3.jar:5.5.3]
        at org.elasticsearch.repositories.blobstore.BlobStoreRepository.finalizeSnapshot(BlobStoreRepository.java:564) ~[elasticsearch-5.5.3.jar:5.5.3]
        at org.elasticsearch.snapshots.SnapshotsService$5.run(SnapshotsService.java:950) [elasticsearch-5.5.3.jar:5.5.3]
        at org.elasticsearch.common.util.concurrent.ThreadContext$ContextPreservingRunnable.run(ThreadContext.java:569) [elasticsearch-5.5.3.jar:5.5.3]
        at java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor.runWorker(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:1142) [?:1.8.0_131]
        at java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor$Worker.run(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:617) [?:1.8.0_131]
        at java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:748) [?:1.8.0_131]
```

### Steps to Reproduce the Problem
Snapshotting ~20 individual indices (1 per action) using the same structure as the snippet shown below. Index sizes range from 200kb - 1.5tb, however the index that failed above had a size of ~15gb.

**Action File Snippet:**
```
SNAPSHOT_DAILY_<redacted>:
  action: snapshot
  description: "daily index: <redacted>"
  options:
    repository: 'repo'
    name: 'snapshot-%Y%m%d%H%M%S_<redacted>'
    ignore_empty_list: True
    continue_if_exception: True
    disable_action: False
  filters:
  - filtertype: kibana
    exclude: True
  - filtertype: period
    source: name
    range_from: -1
    range_to: -1
    timestring: '%Y.%m.%d'
    unit: days
  - filtertype: pattern
    kind: prefix
    value: <redacted>
```

### Specifications
  - Version: 5.5.3
  - Platform: Python 2.7.5 on Rhel7

## Context (Environment)
This issue started showing up once I updated the snapshotting strategy based on structural changes. Went from single cluster single snapshot for all Daily indices (which had high likelihood of causing partial snapshots) to multi-cluster where each cluster had action file with 1 action per daily index (giving more granularity and visibility on snapshots that fail).

issue_title: Cannot create resource groups with parenthesis
issue_body:
```
$ az resource group create -l westus -n "derek(1"
Parameter 'resource_group_name' must conform to the following pattern: '^[-\\w\\._]+$'.
```

From the documentation at https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/dn790546.aspx:
{resource-group-name} must uniquely identify the resource group within the subscription. It must be no longer than 80 characters long. It can only contain alphanumeric characters, dash, underscore, **opening parenthesis, closing parenthesis** or period. The name cannot end with a period. Currently, using a parenthesis in the name causes problems when using Azure PowerShell or Azure CLI. Therefore, we recommend you avoid using an opening or closing parenthesis in the name.

Creating this issue to track this along with xplat CLI and PowerShell.
https://github.com/Azure/azure-xplat-cli/issues/3109
https://github.com/Azure/azure-powershell/issues/2768

issue_title: KNACK ACS Module Conversion
Docstrings for CLI core methods
issue_body:
### Description
`cli_command` and other often used core methods should provide doc string comments. This is particularly important for methods accepting `**kwargs`.

Having this documentation will help developers to understand all of the options available and likely help them to produce better commands. For example, a developer building a command would see they can provide a table transformation with `cli_command`, rather than having to read through the rest of the code to find examples or deep dive into the functionality of the method.

issue_title: Restructure azure-cli package (and command module loading)
issue_body:
The azure-cli package should be a thin entry point to the CLI.
This package will depend on 'azure-cli-core' which holds logic for commands, outputs, etc.
This package can then have dependencies on any command packages that it may need.

Command packages should depend on a package called 'azure-cli-core' to allow command packages to register commands, etc.

This will provide a clear interface between command packages and the core of the CLI.

issue_title: azure-keyvault 1.0.0 doesn't work with MSIAuthentication
issue_body:
It looks like the issue is in KeyVaultAuthentication._init_ method:

if not authorization_callback:
            def auth_callback(server, resource, scope, scheme):
                if self._credentials.resource != resource:
                    self._credentials.resource = resource
                    self._credentials.set_token()

with the code below, self._credentials.resource == resource, so _credentials.set_token() is not called.

Sample code that works with 0.3.7 and doesn't work with 1.0.0:

from azure.keyvault import KeyVaultClient
from msrestazure.azure_active_directory import MSIAuthentication

credentials = MSIAuthentication(resource='https://vault.azure.net')
kvclient = KeyVaultClient(credentials)
res = kvclient.get_secret(url, secret, "").value

issue_title: Low BLOB_MAX_CHUNK_DATA_SIZE leads to performance hits for max_connections=1
issue_body:
### Description:
When using azure.storage.blobservice.get_blob_to_file, the algorithm will download a 64MB (or smaller) file in one call REST call.

However larger files are chunked in 4MB blocks.  For a 65MB file this would result in 17 calls, which significantly decreases performance.  For my application, changing _BLOB_MAX_CHUNK_DATA_SIZE to be the same as _BLOB_MAX_DATA_SIZE (64MB) improved performance drastically.

Not sure of all use cases to know if this will impact other applications, but if it cannot be hard coded to 64MB, perhaps allowing a chunk_size  argument would help.

##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/337
### Comments (1):
#### @huguesv commented on 2015-04-14 22:33:03:
> Thanks for letting us know about this.  Looking at the node storage sdk, it appears that download chunk size may not be limited to 4MB, unless the md5 is requested.  I'll do some testing.

issue_title: Raycast vehicle shaking when resting
issue_body:
In supertuxkart we are using  raycast vehicles from bullet, and I noticed that karts would shake when standing still. Karts would have a linear velocity of around +-0.2 to +-0.3 m/s forward/backward in different frames when no force was applied and the kart were basically at a standstill.

This seems to be caused by the friction handling of the raycast vehicle: calcRollingFriction would return just positive or just negative values.
While I didn't study the physics behind that part of the code, I guessed that because of the comment:
```
        // calculate j that moves us to zero relative velocity
```
it does calculate one impulse to be applied  to slow down the kart's rolling - but is ignoring the fact that there are actually 4 (or whatever) wheels - resulting in a 4-times too large impulse to be applied, causing the kart to reverse direction. So I divided the j1 value returned by calcRollingFriction by the number of wheels, and since then the shaking is basically gone. 
I can't say if this is indeed a bug in the physics, or if I was just lucky that the changed parameter worked in our case :) But I think it's worth having a look.

issue_title: Cleanup after websocket handler cancellation
issue_body:
## Long story short

Is there any pretty way to handle async function after websocket handler cancellation? I've created ugly solutions like:
```
async def wrapper():
    async with request.db_conn.cursor() as cursor:
        await cursor.execute('UPDATE `nodes` SET `status`=%s WHERE `id`=%s', ('DISCONNECTED', node_id))

try:
    await asyncio.shield(wrapper())
except CancelledError:
    pass
```
or putting update action into the event queue handled in different task. 


## Expected behaviour

```
@routes.get('/ws')
async def handler(request):
    logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

    response = web.WebSocketResponse()
    await response.prepare(request)

    async with request.db_conn.cursor() as cursor:
        await cursor.execute('INSERT INTO `clients` (`status`) VALUES (%s);', ('CONNECTED',))
        node_id = cursor.lastrowid

    request.app.websockets[response] = node_id
    logger.debug('Client connected')

    async for msg in response:
        logger.debug(f'Message: {msg}')

        if msg.type == WSMsgType.TEXT:
            data = json.loads(msg.data)
            
        elif msg.type == WSMsgType.ERROR:
            logger.error(f'Connection closed with exception {response.exception()}')

    # CancelledError
    async with request.db_conn.cursor() as cursor:
        await cursor.execute('UPDATE `clients` SET `status`=%s WHERE `id`=%s', ('DISCONNECTED', node_id)) 

    del request.app.websockets[response]
    logger.debug('Client disconnected')

    return response
```

## Actual behaviour

await cursor.execute raises CancelledError

issue_title: Doc clarification -  StreamReader.iter_any() vs iter_chunks()
issue_body:
It might help to clarify in the documentation the difference between [`iter_any`](http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/streams.html#aiohttp.StreamReader.iter_any) and [`iter_chunks`](http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/streams.html#aiohttp.StreamReader.iter_chunks). Currently it says

> Iterates over data chunks in order of intaking them into the stream

> Iterates over data chunks as received from the server

But it's not clear what the difference is between the chunks received from the server and the chunks that were taken into the stream.

My (naive) reading suggests the (almost certainly incorrect) interpretation that `iter_chunks` guarantees to preserve the chunks produced by the server under chunked transfer encoding, and yield them one by one. OTOH, since the `aiohttp` implementation is free to decide how to accept the chunks from the server into the stream (it can combine them or even split them), `iter_any` provides no guarantees about what each yielded chunk is (other than put together they combine to what the server sent).

BTW, this came up when I was reading the discussion about the chunked transfer encoding and `iter_any`, and specifically [this clarification](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/pull/1900#issuecomment-302840811):

> [...] Chunked encoding is just transport protocol only, semantically it's a sequence of bytes without boundaries. Just like a TCP socket is semantically a stream of bytes while the content is transferred by packets. From my perspective partial chunks are totally correct.

issue_title: Multidict preserves order- document it somewhere
issue_body:
As it is implemented now, Multidict preserves order.
Because of the way it is used in aiohttp (parsing post items and HTTP headers), this seems a very desirable property, and not merely an implementation detail.

I think it should be documented both here and in aiohttp's docs that Multidict preserves order, so that one doesn't need to read the implementation to be sure.

issue_title: Make the Gradebook be a context manager
issue_body:
cf #639 

I think this would in general decrease bugs in the future and probably is better practice. It may even catch some bugs we're not aware of!

cc @lgpage

issue_title: Accessing scores per question from assignments
issue_body:
First, a question: Is there a way to export grades, or interact with the gradebook so as to get the grades per assignment, per question? 

As far as I can tell, there is not (although perhaps possible with a custom Exporter?). 

Unless I've missed something that would currently make this possible, I think this might be a useful feature:
- It would allow for better auditing of assignments, seeing where students are having trouble, tracking problem topics, and individual outlier questions (that suggests a problem with the Q).
- It would also be useful to deal grading quirks: it would be easier to recalculate total assignment scores including or excluding certain questions, if issues arise (I have this issue right now in which a bad test case can sometimes fail, and I can't find a clear way to exclude this question from the overall grades).

Perhaps there is something in the underlying grading that makes this type of logging / exporting difficult, but if anyone knows a suggested way to do this, I would like to try it.

issue_body:
Hi,

I got some issues with `%matplotlib notebook` which was discarding my figures when reloading the notebook, and replacing them with `<IPython.core.display.Javascript object>`. I could not figure out why, and I discovered it was due to the fact that my notebook was not trusted.

Thanks

issue_title: d
Could MultiKernelManager connect to kernels started by a different process?
issue_body:
When a request is made to `api/kernels/<kernel_id>`, the `MultiKernelManager` looks in its in-memory cache of known sessions. If it doesn't know about that session, it errors. I am wondering if, before erroring, it could check the on-disk cache of running kernels (e.g., in `~/Library/Jupyter/runtime`). If it find the kernel_id in question, it would start a new session connected to that existing kernel. To say it another way, I see that the cache of kernels known to the manager includes only those [initiated by that particular server process](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/blob/master/jupyter_client/multikernelmanager.py#L105), not any kernels started by some other processes. I'd like the manager to check for kernels started by another process when it doesn't find one of its own matching the requested ID.

Would that be a crazy thing to do? The goal is to enable something like ```ipython console --existing <kernel_id>``` in the notebook server.

For example, suppose Alice and Bob are running separate notebook servers on the same machine. Alice wants to allow Bob to observe the inputs and outputs in a console or notebook that she is working on. She sends Bob the connection file info (perhaps via a hub extension). Bob opens a console and creates a new session connecting to that running kernel. Now Alice and Bob each have a session connected to the same kernel.  Since the JupyterLab console now [mirrors](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyterlab/pull/934#issue-178652109) iopub messages from any session, not just the session that initiated execution, Bob can see Alice's inputs and outputs. He can also execute code. This is one simple mode of real-time collaboration.

issue_title: "Make a copy" no longer works on read-only notebooks
issue_body:
When editing a read-only jupyter notebook (made read-only via file system) I noticed that I can no longer use the "Make a copy" feature. All that happens is that a new empty tab is opened. 

I played a bit around to find out when this happens exactly. 

1. One can get lucky if one is quick enough to hit the button before Jupyter is finished rendering the notebook. 
 2. I could only reproduce this issue when the Jupyter Notebook is served via a port reroute (using iptables from port 443 or 80 to 8888) via web. On a local installation this does not happen. 
 
As a note this did not use to be an issue like half a year ago were I ran the same set of Jupyter Notebooks with the exact same set up. I can be sure of that since I used the same docker setup.

I can supply a running live server where this can tested out if needed.

issue_title: Improve keyboard shortcut accessibility

The keyboard shortcut are really useful, but because they're not very standard (not many other apps do or can share shortcuts with these notebooks, because they're so different), they're hard to remember.

- Add a filter bar to the  `help > keyboard shortcuts` dialogue, such that if you type a few letters, the dialogue filters out all items that don't contain that string.

issue_title: rfftn and irfftn
issue_body:
The current fft module does not support real-complex fourier transforms.

It is not very clear what to do in this case, because the current convention says the gradient of FFT is FFT itself. However, for real-complex transforms the vjd functions demand a different function signature:

- rfft: real -> complex
- vjd(rfft) : complex->real

issue_title: Calls to logger should use Error instead of Debug

issue_title: Writing a modified BibTeX
issue_body:
Hi,

As is, if I understood correctly, the writer has to take a `BibDatabase` object. Then, I can simply:

```python3
bib_db = bibtexparser.loads(SOME_BIBTEX)
bib_db.entries[0]["SOME_FIELD"] = SOME_NEW_VALUE
bibtexparser.dumps(bib_db)
```
and it works fine.

Now, the tricky part is when I want to do it with a part of a BibTeX file. Say I have a BibTeX file with two entries, and want to extract a single entry. I can

```python3
bib_db = bibtexparser.loads(SOME_BIBTEX)
entry = bib_db.entries[SOME_ID]
```

But my `entry` is a `dict` and not a `BibDatabase` object anymore. So I cannot write it any longer.

For me, there is an inconsistency here in the API, and there should be either a function to extract a `BibDatabase` object from a subset of a BibTeX file, or recreate a BibDatabase object from an entry dict, or each entry should itself be a `BibDatabase` object, or something like this. But perhaps I missed something…

Thanks!

issue_title: "sshuttle -D" silently fails if pid file exists
issue_body:
Using 0.78.1 on macos. (Thanks, sshuttle comes in really handy for making a docker container network accessible outside of docker.)

I use below command to start a "vpn" to the REAL_SERVER ip address, via ssh to a machine that has access to REAL_SERVER.
```
$ sshuttle -D --remote ${SSH_PROXY_USER}@${SSH_PROXY_HOST}:${SSH_PROXY_PORT} ${REAL_SERVER}
```

I couldn't figure out why this would sometimes silently fail to do anything. I expected a "sudo" prompt and the network routes to be created. Turns out it is failing when I have already started an unrelated sshuttle session. 

**Seems failures before daemonization should log to console, not only to syslog.** And is there a way to disable syslog, log to stderr instead? Finally, since it seems multiple sessions are allowed and work fine, maybe the pid file should be uniquely named and maybe put into a shared directory so it's easy to find and kill these sessions.

PS: I shouldn't say "silent" fails because this message _does_ go to syslog (the Utilities => Console app in macos), but it's easy to miss
```
/Users/.../sshuttle.pid: sshuttle is already running (pid=4601)
```

issue_title: max parameter on list() methods doesn't work
issue_body:
The max parameter does not actually limit the number of items retrieved.

The reason for this seems to be lines 79 through 90 in restsession.py

After calling requests.get() [line 77], the response is checked and yielded [lines 81 and 82]. But then the next page is requested and assigned to the response variable [lines 84 through 87], and the while loop [line 79] reprocesses the variable.

A simple solution would be to remove the loop altogether, leaving just lines 80 through 82; but I don't know if the paged behaviour is needed by other methods.

issue_title: A new method named `modify` needs to be a selective operation.
issue_body:
Along with resolution of #608 resolution of this issue is intended to resolve #564. 

The new method `replace` will replace the old update method.    The new `update` will be selective.

Instead of `put`ing the whole configuration object in a request to the server, `update` will only submit the explicitly specified parameters in its kwargs, in a `patch` request to the server.   This feature should be implemented _after_ #608.

Once this feature/issue, _AND_ #608 have successfully landed we should revisit #564.

issue_title: Adding new *.cs file while having the property EnableDefaultCompileItems on false
issue_body:
# Creating new *.cs file while having the property EnableDefaultCompileItems on false does not automatically add this file in the project file

# General

- This is on Visual Studio 2017 Version 15.5.57
- Using the new SDK project format style
- Has `<EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems>`
- Has external files included using the `<Compile Include="" />` way
- Project was ported from the old .net framework project style to the new style as this project is now a netstandard2.0 project.

I can actually fix this issue very easily by just removing the <EnableDefaultCompileItems>false</EnableDefaultCompileItems> and removing the two files that are already included with the `<Compile Include="" />` 

However some solutions that we have, have cs files that are compiled with a Prebuild target. To use them while building, they needed  to be explicitly added in the project file and therefor the EnableDefaultCompileItems property was also used.

issue_title: Having a way to e-mail user about certificate status, renewal, etc.
issue_body:
We've talked about this a bit. We sometimes want to tell the user about things that have happened that relate to a certificate: for example, the fact that it's about to expire, will be renewed automatically, has been renewed automatically, will be installed automatically, has been installed automatically, has been revoked, could not be renewed, or could not be installed.

@pde suggested not using the recovery contact information to send such notifications from the CA side, but I don't think we've definitively decided whether the CA side can ever send automated e-mail to anyone about certificates. The client can definitely *try*, and it will definitely try, but it's in a worse position in terms of likelihood of success: it may not be able to send mail to `root@localhost` at all, and even if it does so, there may be no one reading that e-mail!

What's the best way to handle all of these things? I wanted to wrap `/usr/sbin/sendmail` (haven't found a convenient and maintained library for doing so); is speaking SMTP to `localhost:25` (say via Python's `smtplib`) as likely to work, more likely to work, or less likely to work?

Should we default to trying to e-mail `webmaster@example.com` on top of `root@localhost`? Or `webmaster@localhost`? (We can have an optional configuration file parameter for e-mail addresses to notify, but I don't expect it to be prompted for during the original cert deployment process, and most people won't have set it subsequently.)

issue_title: TopicPartition_str0 broken on Mac OS X
issue_body:
This one is minor but annoying...

On Mac OS X 10.10.5, Python 2.7.9:
```
>>> from confluent_kafka import TopicPartition
>>> print TopicPartition('topic')
TopicPartition{topic=topic,partition=-1,offset=%lld,error=%s}
```

On Ubuntu Python 2.7.6
```
>>> from confluent_kafka import TopicPartition
>>> print TopicPartition('topic')
TopicPartition{topic=topic,partition=-1,offset=-1001,error=None}

```

I tried to debug this a little and the issue seems to be that mac generates "%lld" and ubuntu generates "%ld" from "PRId64".  PyUnicode_FromFormat (v2) does not support "%lld" (was added in v3.2)

```
mac$ /usr/bin/clang -E -fno-strict-aliasing -fno-common -dynamic -arch x86_64 -g -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -I/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/include/python2.7 -c confluent_kafka/src/confluent_kafka.c |grep "TopicPartition{"
 ret = PyUnicodeUCS2_FromFormat("TopicPartition{topic=%s,partition=%""d" ",offset=%""ll" "d"",error=%s}", self->topic, self->partition, self->offset, errstr ? PyString_AsString(PyUnicodeUCS2_AsUTF8String(errstr)) : "None");

ubuntu$ x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc -E -pthread -fno-strict-aliasing -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O2 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -fPIC -I/usr/include/python2.7 -c confluent_kafka/src/confluent_kafka.c|grep "TopicPartition{"
 ret = PyUnicodeUCS4_FromFormat("TopicPartition{topic=%s,partition=%""d" ",offset=%""l" "d"",error=%s}", self->topic, self->partition, self->offset, errstr ? PyString_AsString(PyUnicodeUCS4_AsUTF8String(errstr)) : "None")

```

    return self._cachecomp[self._cachedate.index((dt, self._fold(dt)))]

``_cachedate`` and ``_cachecomp`` are not protected by a lock.

issue_title: Release 2.5.0
issue_body:
I've been really negligent in getting this release out, I think it's time to wrap up whatever work needs to be done and get a release out ASAP. I'm creating this issue to discuss exactly what should be included in 2.5.0 and what should be pushed to later releases.

**2.5.0**:
-  tzwin fixes: ✓
    - PR #142 ✓
    - tzname() inconsistent between tzwin() and tzwinlocal() and neither reflect actual DST (#143) (PR #178) ✓
    - tzwinlocal's repr is incorrect (#148) (PR #179 -> #184) ✓
    - tzwin for the same timezone string does not compare equal (#151) (PR #180 -> #184) ✓
- tz fixes: ✓
    - dateutil does not accept a dt argument of None (#132) (PR #181) ✓
    - tz submodule not created by setup.py install (#165) ✓ 
- RRule
    - heapq performance enhancement - PR #136 ✓
- Parser:
    - Move `smart_defaults` back into a feature branch (#30) - in the time since adding this, I've been thinking we may want to implement this differently, and I don't want to get stuck maintaining API compatibility. (This should automatically fix #126 for the release, but that should also be updated on the feature branch). I think this should be re-implemented with a combination of #125 and breaking up `parse` into smaller, customizable functions. (PR #191) ✓
    - PR #130 (properly handle dates prior to year 100) ✓
    - Issue #78 (raise error on empty string) (PR #187) ✓
- Relativedelta:
    - Issue #40 - This is mostly implemented, I'll clean up and make PR. (PR #190) ✓
- build:
    - Either fix `updatezinfo` so that it doesn't fail so much or add some fallback logic to the build. (#176)  ✓ 

Things to push to 2.5.1:
- Issue #70 (API changes - don't want to rush)
- Issue #79 (I have this half-done, but it's a bit complicated to do so I need to add tests and I don't want to put things off)
- tzwin does not accept some values returned by tzname (#144)
- Issue #145 - This is apparently a big problem for at least one project, so we don't want it to persist through two releases. (Seems to have been mostly resolved on their side, so we can push it to 2.5.1)

@jarondl Anything critical I'm missing?

issue_title: 'format' key in input YAML interpreted as file
issue_body:
When the input file is a YAML that contains a key named 'format', then it is interpreted as file.

Workflow, echo.cwl:
```
cwlVersion: v1.0
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo

inputs:
  format:
    type: string
    inputBinding:
      position: 1

outputs:
    output: stdout
```

Input YAML, hello_world.yaml:
```
format: Hello world
```

Usage:
```
cwltool echo.cwl hello_world.yaml
```

## Expected Behavior
The output file should contain the string: 'Hello world'.
Similar to cwltool echo.cwl --format 'Hello world'

## Actual Behavior

Hi,

```python
@swagger.map_to_swagger_type('file', None)

But in order to make swagger-ui actually work i need to set:
`{'consumes': ['multipart/form-data', 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded']}`
on my post operation.

fix = spec.to_dict()['paths']['/projects/{id}/image']['post']

```

Is there a better way to do this?

issue_title: Unenveloping None values doesn't work as expected
issue_body:
Trying to unenvelope serialized data when the contained data is None continues deserialization with the original data. This makes it impossible to deserialize nested fields with enveloped data where None is a valid value. (Related: http://jsonapi.org/format/)

Example (let's pretend our system allows for homeless employees):
https://gist.github.com/tdevelioglu/8e433355494ba022f48f
(I expect a deserialized Resource object, with an attribute 'address' with value 'None')

issue_title: Proposal: load_from lookup for dotted path
issue_body:
It would be extremely useful to have dotted-lookup for loading objects, like it template engines for example.
Consider the following code:
```python
In [5]: class TestSchema(ma.Schema):
   ...:     fld_count = ma.fields.Str(load_from='fld.count', required=True)
   ...:     first_item_color = ma.fields.Str(load_from='items.0.color', required=True)
   ...:     

In [6]: TestSchema().load({'fld': {'count': 1}, 'items': [{'color': 'red'}]})
Out[6]: UnmarshalResult(data={}, errors={'items.0.color': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'fld.count': ['Missing data for required field.']})
```

There is some caveats, for example when dealing with dictionary with dotted key of same name - lookup will be ambiguous, but i don't think this can be a big problem. But if you consider this as backward-compatibility problem, option can be named "load_from_path".
I understand that we can use fields.Nested, but it will be problem if we want several fields on root schema from same object in nested schema.
Also I understand that we can use `@pre_load`, but it's obvious better to keep things in declarative way.
Should we extend `load_from`, or create new option for this? Should I create pull request? Thank you.

issue_title: can I read a csv with marshmallow??
issue_body:
Hi, i was trying to read a csv file, but i can't find a solution, so, i wrote this little method. i don't know if works really good and i would like to help me to improve it.

**Code to insert in BaseSchema class**

```python
def csv(self, data, many=None, partial=None):
        from itertools import izip_longest
        many = self.many if self.many is not None else (False if many is None else True)

        if many == True:
            data = [dict(izip_longest(self.opts.fields, d)) for d in data]
        else:
            data = dict(izip_longest(self.opts.fields,data))

        result, errors = self._do_load(data, many, partial=partial, postprocess=True)
        return UnmarshalResult(data=result, errors=errors)
```
**Code Example**

issue_title: Add option to throw validation error on extra keys
issue_body:
I'm not sure if I'm missing something, but I would like to throw error if extra keys are provided, as in:

``` python
>>> class AlbumSchema(Schema):
...     title = fields.Str()

>>> AlbumSchema(strict=True).load({'extra': 2}, allow_extra=False)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
marshmallow.exceptions.ValidationError: {'_schema': ["Extra arguments passed: ['extra']"]}
```

I've been using the following implementation (taken from https://github.com/sloria/webargs/issues/87#issuecomment-183949558):

``` python
class BaseSchema(Schema):

    @pre_load
    def validate_extra(self, in_data):
        if not isinstance(in_data, dict):
            return

        extra_args = [key for key in in_data.keys() if key not in self.fields]
        if extra_args:
            raise ValidationError('Extra arguments passed: {}'.format(extra_args))
```

I would expect this to be a common need, however, so could it be supported by the library out-of-the-box?

issue_title: Clarification: Providing a custom dict class to Schema
issue_body:
Just wanting to see if I'm understanding this correctly: If I need to provide a custom mapping class to put the serialized data into, I should override the `dict_class` property on Schema. 

A simple example: Using Marshmallow to serialize response for display in a View.

```python
class DottedDict(dict):
    "Preserve dotted access to pretend we're accessing the original object"
    __getattr__ = dict.__getitem__


# use this class as the base for all my schema
class ViewModelSchema(Schema):
    dict_class = DottedDict

# when dump is called, result.data will be a DottedDict
class UserSchema(ViewModelSchema):
    class Meta:
        fields = ['username', 'email', 'registered_at']
```

Is this considered a "public API" or am I backdooring. I understand that setting `ordered` wouldn't affect anything now -- but that's not a concern if I'm simply serializing data down to use in a template instead of shoving it off into a JSON API.


If this is a public API, would a PR for documentation on this be accepted?

issue_title: Extend LightFM with ANN ordering for recommendations
issue_body:
Considering the following:
- Practically all implementations will not just implement the LightFM model but also an ANN ordering to get to the 'top x results'. 
- Due to the dot product equation in LightFM not all ANN algorithms are suitable/ easy to implement. Issues to tackle for instance are: is the ANN ordering method applicable, should biases be discarded y/n, normalise item and user vectors y/n?
- The ANN benchmark (https://github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks) has a clear outperformer, i.e. hsnw (as part of the nsmlib) which does support (approximate) inner product search and thus suits LightFM wonderfully.

Could you extend LightFM to have an ANN ordering (preferably hsnw)? Give more guidance and details on how go about in getting recommendations out of LightFM. That will seriously benefit most users of this great algorithm you made.
